blurb:
Eugene has a whole system for deciding if he likes you. First he watches from a distance. Then he walks slow circles, getting a little closer each time. Eventually he brushes up against your leg, and that's his way of saying you passed. He's around 7 years old, a little shy, and absolutely worth the patience it takes to earn his trust.

Once he's comfortable, he's the kind of cat who will sit next to you on the couch and eventually migrate into your lap. You'll know he's fully relaxed when his tongue pokes out a little. His purr is loud. He makes biscuits. He'll groom and chase a buddy around the house if you have another cat or a dog.

He does need space to work through the getting-to-know-you phase, so a house with room to observe from a distance is important. He's had a hard time in apartments and does much better where he has room to breathe and settle in on his own timeline. He's not a pick-him-up-whenever cat, but when he decides he wants your lap, he'll let you know. Submit an adoption application at threepawsrescue.org to get started.

**Three Paws Rescue**
Chamblee, GA
www.threepawsrescue.org
scoring_notes:
• Needs patient adopter with space — a narrower audience, but the right match will feel personally chosen by him
• His methodical trust-building ritual is a charming, relatable hook that will resonate with introverted readers
• Specific needs (no apartments, no forced handling) suggest multiple placement attempts, hinting at a longer wait

blurb:
Junior is a black Lab who spent years in a home before his family decided they were suddenly allergic and dropped him at the county shelter. He sat there for a long time while people walked past. Black dog syndrome is a real thing, and Junior felt every bit of it.

Here's what you should actually know about Junior: the dog is a regular at Bruster's and Pelican's. Ice cream outings are apparently a staple of his social life. He's house trained, well mannered, and when he gets to spend time in a home with his favorite volunteer Katie, he thrives. The shelter dog version of Junior and the home version of Junior are two very different experiences.

He does best as the only dog and prefers a house with older kids. If that's your situation, he's going to be the most loyal dog you've ever had. He's already proven he can be a great companion. He just needs someone to give him the chance to show it again.

Our Pal's Place is on Canton Road in Marietta. Call or email to set up a visit.
scoring_notes:
• Black dog syndrome named explicitly in the blurb transforms a systemic adoption barrier into a compelling reader education moment that drives empathy and action
• Years in a home, then surrendered and overlooked — the contrast between who Junior was and who he can be again is one of the most resonant arcs in this set
• Explicit reference to sitting at the county shelter "for a long time" while people walked past is the clearest direct evidence of an extended wait among all candidates

blurb:
Rudolph spent the first year of his life in a hoarding situation in South Georgia, one of seven dogs pulled from the same place. He came in unsure of himself and probably had no idea what it felt like to just be a regular, playful dog. That has changed. His favorite thing now is running zoomies in the yard with his foster sister Isabelle, and he has completely come out of his shell.

He's about a year old, gets along great with other dogs and cats, and is already crate trained. His brother Rudy came from the same rescue and they apparently look so alike it's hard to tell them apart.

Rudolph does best in a home with at least one other adult dog and a fenced yard. Rescue Me GA is also upfront that while he's a poodle mix, they don't know how much poodle is actually in him, so allergy-friendly is not guaranteed. If you've got experience with doodle-type dogs and room to run, apply at rmgdogs.org or email adrienne@rescuemega.org.
scoring_notes:
• A one-year-old who went from hoarding chaos to yard zoomies is a feel-good turnaround story with broad, accessible appeal
• The twin-dog angle with brother Rudy adds a charming human-interest layer that stands out in a crowded adoption feed
• Younger age and strong social skills with dogs and cats suggest he is the most recently available candidate with the shortest likely wait

blurb:
When the intake team at Our Pal's Place pulled Otis off the county kill list, they found a dog so overwhelmed by the noise and overcrowding that he was shaking in his own cell. The moment they got him into a quiet room, he walked straight over and buried his head in someone's lap. That was all he needed. Just out.

Since arriving at Our Pal's Place, Otis has turned into a completely different dog. Friendly, tail-wagging, happy to sit politely and soak up every bit of attention he can get. He remembered his house training almost immediately once the stress lifted. The volunteers describe him as loyal and loving, the kind of dog who just wants to be near his people and make them happy.

His story is a hard one, but he came out of it with a good heart intact. Whoever adopts Otis isn't just giving a dog a home. They're getting a dog who already knows what it felt like to almost lose everything, and who will not take a single good day for granted.

Our Pal's Place is on Canton Road in Marietta. Reach out by phone or email to get the process started.
scoring_notes:
• The kill-list rescue followed by an immediate lap moment is the strongest single emotional hook in this entire candidate set — built for reader impact
• His rapid behavioral turnaround in a calm environment makes him a genuinely easy adoption pitch with broad household appeal
• Pulled directly off a county kill list signals he was deep in the system before rescue, implying the longest effective wait time of any candidate

blurb:
When Morrie arrived at his foster home last fall, he was in rough shape. Severely underweight, bad skin infection, clearly neglected for a long time. The rescue estimates he spent years like that. He's an Italian Greyhound, somewhere around nine or ten years old, and he had basically never known what it felt like to be taken care of.

That was several months ago. He's now 18 pounds, healthy, and apparently hilarious. His foster mom says he's goofy and spunky and regularly makes her laugh. He gets along great with other dogs and seems to be making up for lost time.

Morrie is a senior dog who has genuinely never had a person of his own. He's not looking for much. Just someone to come home to. If you have a quiet home and want a small, funny, grateful little guy in it, email adrienne@rescuemega.org or apply at rmgdogs.org.
scoring_notes:
• A senior dog who has never had a person of his own is one of the most emotionally potent hooks in rescue writing — impossible to read without feeling something
• The contrast between years of neglect and his current goofy, healthy personality gives readers a clear before-and-after they want to be part of
• Nine to ten years old, arrived last fall, and still waiting — the longest probable shelter timeline in this group by a meaningful margin

blurb:
She came to rescue as part of a court case, with scars that tell a story nobody wants to hear. But that chapter is closed. Panda is a seven-year-old mixed breed who has since beaten heartworm disease, raised eight puppies, and come out the other side ready to find a couch to call her own.

She's got a lot of life left in her. In her foster home she runs and plays with dogs half her age and keeps right up with them. She's great with all people and loves a good romp followed by a long nap. She does best with bigger dogs as her companions.

Panda has been through more than most dogs ever will. What she needs now is simple: a patient person, a comfortable spot to sleep, and a big dog friend to play with. If that sounds like your house, Rescue Me GA wants to hear from you. Fill out an application at rmgdogs.org or email adrienne@rescuemega.org.
scoring_notes:
• Court case origin, heartworm survival, and eight puppies raised — her résumé of hardship and resilience is unmatched in this group
• A seven-year-old dog who keeps pace with younger dogs dismantles the senior-pet stigma in a single sentence
• Senior age plus a history of trauma and a companion-dog requirement suggest she has been waiting significantly longer than average

blurb:
She was found outside an apartment complex with a litter of kittens and no one looking for her. The kittens have all been adopted. Now it's her turn. Mandy is about a year and a half old, Maine Coon mix, and genuinely one of the more easygoing cats you'll meet. She rolls onto her back and lets you pet her belly. Not many cats do that.

She's FIV positive, which sounds more alarming than it is. FIV is only transmitted between cats through a deep bite wound. Not through shared bowls, shared litter boxes, or living in the same house. Plenty of FIV positive cats live alongside FIV negative cats without any issue. It changes nothing about her day-to-day life or how affectionate and gentle she is.

This is a cat who raised kittens on the street and still came out sweet and trusting. That says a lot about her. Patty's Kitties is based in Atlanta. Reach out by phone or email to ask about Mandy and get the adoption process started.

**Patty's Kitties**
Atlanta, GA 30319
(404) 281-9035 | pms0702@comcast.net
scoring_notes:
• Her kittens all found homes while she waits — a built-in emotional gut-punch that drives reader urgency
• FIV stigma is almost certainly why she's still available, making her a perfect candidate for myth-busting newsletter content
• Street-raised litter, now belly-up trusting strangers — her arc is the most compelling redemption story in this batch

blurb:
Luke's story starts with a tough one. His mom, Miss Daisy, lost her owner unexpectedly, and when no family stepped up, she and her two puppies ended up at the county shelter. Luke and his brother Bo sat there for weeks before a rescue finally pulled them out.

Now at Our Pal's Place, Luke has had a pretty good run. He arrived right in the middle of the shelter's summer youth camp, which meant trail walks, kids, activity, and a lot of attention. He is playful and loving, with a face that leans more Malinois than German Shepherd, though he's smaller than you might expect from either. The volunteers describe him as a fun-loving boy who just wants to find his person.

He's young, so he's going to need some time and consistency as he figures out what home life looks like. But the foundation is solid. Good energy, good heart, good dog.

Our Pal's Place is on Canton Road in Marietta. Call or email to ask about Luke and set up a visit.
scoring_notes:
• The multigenerational rescue angle — mom Miss Daisy loses her owner, puppies follow her into the system — adds rare emotional depth and family-story appeal
• Summer camp socialization with kids and trail activity paints an unusually vivid, positive picture that makes him easy for readers to visualize in their own lives
• "Weeks" at the county shelter before rescue is explicitly stated, providing a concrete time anchor, though his overall wait appears shorter than the senior dog candidates

blurb:
She started at Good Mews as a kitten, got adopted, and then somehow ended up scared and alone at a county shelter. Nobody knows what happened in between. But Good Mews got the call and brought her straight back, and after some time in a foster home, Beignet is doing really well.

She was shy at first, which makes sense given what she went through. But she settled in fast and her real personality came out. She likes being petted, loves having company while she eats, and when she wants to be close to you, she'll lean right up against you. It's a pretty good way to end up with a cat in your lap.

One thing worth knowing: Beignet can give a little love bite when she gets overstimulated. It's not aggression, just her way of saying she's had enough for now. Someone who pays attention to cat body language will get along with her great. She's already been through enough uncertainty. She deserves someone who's going to stick around.

Good Mews is a cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. Submit an inquiry through their site or reach out by phone or email to get the process started.
scoring_notes:
• Full-circle rescue narrative — adopted, lost, recovered — gives readers an emotionally satisfying story with a clear call to action
• Minor love-bite caveat is honestly disclosed but framed as manageable, keeping her broadly appealing to cat-savvy adopters
• Second stint in shelter care after a failed adoption signals she's been waiting longer than a typical cat, adding urgency

blurb:
These two came in together from a county shelter, and they're staying together. Midna and her brother Frankenwhisker are a bonded pair, which means they need to be adopted as a package deal. If you've ever watched two cats absolutely lose their minds wrestling each other at 11pm, you already know what you're signing up for.

Midna is the shyer of the two. New environments rattle her, and she'll need someone who's patient enough to let her come around at her own pace. She's not going to be greeting you at the door on day one. But once she settles in and builds some trust, she'll be a solid companion. Frankenwhisker is there to help with that process, and honestly, having a familiar face around makes the transition a lot easier for cats like her.

Two cats sounds like more work, but with a bonded pair, they keep each other company and burn off energy together. Your furniture takes the hit instead of your sanity.

Good Mews is a cage-free shelter on Robinson Road in Marietta. Contact them by phone or email to ask about Midna and Frankenwhisker and set up a time to meet them both.
scoring_notes:
• Bonded-pair requirement cuts the adopter pool significantly, making this the hardest placement in the list and the most newsletter-worthy as a challenge story
• "Frankenwhisker" is an editorial gift — the name alone will stop a reader mid-scroll and generate clicks and shares
• No strong signals of an unusually long wait, but the bonded-pair constraint inherently extends time in care, warranting moderate urgency framing

blurb:
His name is Pork Tenderloin and yes, that's really what it says on his paperwork. He came to Good Mews from a county shelter, and if you spend five minutes reading about him, it's pretty clear he just hasn't found his person yet.

He's affectionate, but on his own schedule. He'll come find you when he wants attention, and when he does, it's genuinely sweet. He's not a lap cat who tolerates everything thrown at him. He has preferences and he'll let you know what they are. Some people find that kind of cat easier to live with than one who demands constant interaction.

He does get overwhelmed faster than some cats, so a calm home suits him better than a chaotic one. Someone who knows how to read a cat and won't push past his limits will get the best version of him. In the right situation, he's going to be a really solid companion.

Good Mews is on Robinson Road in Marietta. Reach out by phone or email to start the process and set up a visit.
scoring_notes:
• Memorable name alone earns a second look and drives newsletter open rates better than almost any other candidate here
• Blurb honestly positions him for a specific adopter type, narrowing the pool but increasing match quality and long-term placement success
• "Hasn't found his person yet" phrasing implies an extended wait at the county shelter before Good Mews, suggesting meaningful time in the system

blurb:
Brushing her head and neck is basically a cheat code. Brie is five years old, orange and white, medium-length fur, and a dedicated purring machine the moment you pick up a brush. She's a little shy when you first meet her, but once she decides you're okay, she'll park herself next to your laptop while you work and politely tap your hand when she wants attention.

She does have a few preferences worth knowing about. She's not a fan of other cats and does best as the only one in the house, or with a cat who genuinely wants nothing to do with her. She gets along fine with dogs that give her space. And because she can get overwhelmed, she'd do best in a quieter home with adults or older teens rather than a busy, loud household.

She's currently around 14.5 lbs and does well with scheduled, portioned meals rather than free feeding. Nothing complicated, just a routine she can count on. If you work from home and want a calm, affectionate companion who earns her keep in purrs, Brie would fit right in. Fill out an adoption application at threepawsrescue.org to get started.

**Three Paws Rescue**
Chamblee, GA
www.threepawsrescue.org
scoring_notes:
• The work-from-home angle is a highly targeted, modern hook that maps perfectly to a large segment of newsletter readers
• No dramatic backstory, but her specific and relatable quirks (laptop companion, brush obsession) make her easy to picture at home
• Solo-cat requirement and household noise sensitivity suggest she's been passed over, supporting a moderate wait time
